panel.origin: The Dungeon Master — the player who runs the game, narrates the world, controls non-player characters, and adjudicates the rules. Part storyteller, part referee.
panel.mixology: The DM described the torchlit dungeon and asked what the party wanted to do next.
panel.examples: From 'Dungeons & Dragons', the original game. Other systems call this role the Game Master (GM) or Storyteller, but the DM's job — running the world — is the same.

panel.origin: The polyhedral dice that drive the game's randomness. The twenty-sided 'd20' is iconic, but sets include d4, d6, d8, d10, and d12 too.
panel.mixology: She scooped up her dice and rolled the d20, holding her breath as it tumbled.
panel.examples: 'Dice' is ancient, but the polyhedral set is RPG signature. 'd20' means 'a die with 20 sides' — the 'd' notation (d6, d8) is universal tabletop shorthand.

panel.origin: Rolling dice to determine an outcome. A 'check' tests a skill against a target number — the core 'roll the dice and add your bonus' resolution of the game.
panel.mixology: He had to roll a Stealth check to sneak past the sleeping guard.
panel.examples: From the everyday 'roll' of dice. The 'roll plus modifier versus a target' mechanic is the heartbeat of most tabletop RPGs — chance shaped by character ability.

panel.origin: Difficulty Class — the target number a player must meet or beat on a roll to succeed at a task. The DM sets the DC based on how hard the action is.
panel.mixology: The DM set a DC of 15 for the tricky lock, and her roll just cleared it.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'class' (level) of 'difficulty'. A higher DC means a harder task — the simple dial a DM turns to make challenges easy or daunting.

panel.origin: 'Advantage' lets you roll two dice and take the higher; 'disadvantage' makes you take the lower. An elegant way to reflect favorable or unfavorable circumstances.
panel.mixology: Flanking the enemy, she attacked with advantage, rolling twice and keeping the better result.
panel.examples: A signature mechanic of D&D's fifth edition, replacing fiddly bonuses with a clean roll-twice system that intuitively models good and bad situations.

panel.origin: A number added to or subtracted from a dice roll, based on a character's abilities, skills, or circumstances. Your modifiers turn a raw roll into a result.
panel.mixology: With a +5 modifier to Athletics, his climbing rolls were reliably strong.
panel.examples: From 'modify' — the bonus or penalty 'modifies' the raw die. Modifiers are how a character's training and talent tilt the odds in their favor (or against).

panel.origin: A 'natural 20' is rolling a 20 on the d20 before modifiers — often a critical success. A 'natural 1' is the opposite: an automatic, often comical failure.
panel.mixology: She rolled a nat 20 on the impossible leap, and the whole table cheered.
panel.examples: 'Natural' means the die's raw face value, unmodified. The nat 20 (triumph) and nat 1 (disaster) are tabletop's most dramatic, celebrated, and dreaded rolls.

panel.origin: An ongoing series of connected game sessions forming a continuous story. A campaign can span months or years as characters grow and the plot unfolds.
panel.mixology: Their campaign had run for two years, the characters evolving from nobodies to legendary heroes.
panel.examples: From the military sense of a 'campaign' (a connected series of operations). It's the long-form story arc that distinguishes RPGs from one-off board games.

panel.origin: A character's profession or archetype — Fighter, Wizard, Rogue, Cleric — defining their abilities, role, and play style. The core of who your character is.
panel.mixology: He chose the Wizard class, trading toughness for a spellbook full of devastating magic.
panel.examples: From 'class' (a category or type). A character's class determines their powers and role in the party — the fundamental build choice in most RPGs.

panel.origin: A character's species or ancestry — Human, Elf, Dwarf, Halfling — granting distinct traits and flavor. Many games now use 'species' or 'ancestry' instead.
panel.mixology: She played a Dwarf, gaining darkvision and a sturdy resistance to poison.
panel.examples: From 'race' in the older sense of a kind or lineage. Modern games increasingly prefer 'species' or 'ancestry' to describe a character's heritage and innate traits.

panel.origin: A measure of a character's power and experience. Characters 'level up' by earning experience points, gaining new abilities and growing stronger over time.
panel.mixology: After defeating the dragon, the party leveled up, unlocking powerful new abilities.
panel.examples: From 'level' (a rank or stage). Leveling up — the satisfying climb from weak novice to mighty hero — is one of the RPG's most rewarding loops.

panel.origin: The six core statistics defining a character: Strength, Dexterity, Constitution, Intelligence, Wisdom, and Charisma. They underpin nearly every roll.
panel.mixology: He dumped his Charisma to boost Strength, building a mighty but socially hopeless warrior.
panel.examples: The numerical foundation of a character. These six scores (often rolled or assigned at creation) feed the modifiers behind almost every action in the game.

panel.origin: Hit Points — a measure of how much damage a character can take before falling unconscious or dying. HP drops as you're hurt and is restored by rest or healing.
panel.mixology: Down to her last few hit points, she desperately quaffed a healing potion.
panel.examples: From the sense of how many 'hits' a character can take. HP is the universal health bar of RPGs — reach zero and your hero is in mortal trouble.

panel.origin: Armor Class — a number representing how hard a character is to hit. An attacker must roll equal to or above your AC to land a blow. Higher AC means tougher to strike.
panel.mixology: The knight's heavy plate gave him an armor class so high most blows simply glanced off.
panel.examples: Descriptive: the 'class' (rating) of one's 'armor' and defenses. AC is the target number attackers must beat — the core of whether a hit connects.

panel.origin: A character's moral and ethical outlook, plotted on two axes (good–evil, lawful–chaotic). 'Chaotic Good' or 'Lawful Evil' sketch a character's guiding principles.
panel.mixology: Her Chaotic Good rogue stole from tyrants but always shared the spoils with the poor.
panel.examples: A nine-box grid from early D&D crossing law-vs-chaos with good-vs-evil. A shorthand (and endless debate topic) for a character's moral compass.

panel.origin: A roll at the start of combat that determines turn order. Higher initiative acts first — crucial for getting your blows in before the enemy.
panel.mixology: She rolled high on initiative and struck first, before the goblins could even react.
panel.examples: From 'initiative' (the right to begin). The initiative roll sets the sequence of a battle, deciding who seizes the first, often decisive, move.

panel.origin: On your turn, you typically get one main 'action' (attack, cast a spell) plus possibly a 'bonus action' for quicker moves. Managing your actions is core to combat tactics.
panel.mixology: He used his action to attack and his bonus action to dash out of danger.
panel.examples: From 'action' (a thing done). The action economy — what you can do in a single turn — is the tactical puzzle at the heart of RPG combat.

panel.origin: A limited resource representing a spellcaster's daily magical energy. Casting a spell expends a slot of the appropriate level, recovered after a rest.
panel.mixology: Down to her last spell slot, the wizard saved it for an emergency.
panel.examples: Descriptive: a 'slot' that holds a spell's energy. The system limits how much magic a caster can unleash before resting, forcing careful resource management.

panel.origin: A minor spell that can be cast at will, without using a spell slot. Cantrips are a caster's reliable, unlimited basic magic.
panel.mixology: Out of spell slots, the wizard fell back on his trusty Fire Bolt cantrip.
panel.examples: An old word for a magic trick or charm (from Scots). In RPGs, cantrips are the simplest spells — weak but endlessly repeatable, a caster's basic attack.

panel.origin: A roll to resist or avoid a harmful effect — dodging a fireball, shrugging off poison, resisting a charm. Your last line of defense against danger.
panel.mixology: He made his saving throw against the dragon's breath, taking only half the damage.
panel.examples: From 'save' (to avoid harm). The saving throw is the defender's roll — a chance to 'save' yourself from spells, traps, and effects that would otherwise hit full force.

panel.origin: Area of Effect — a spell or attack that strikes everything in a region, like a fireball engulfing a room. AoE rewards catching enemies bunched together.
panel.mixology: The wizard dropped a fireball, an area of effect that caught the whole cluster of orcs.
panel.examples: Borrowed from broader gaming. An 'area of effect' spell hits a zone rather than one target — devastating against grouped foes, risky near allies.

panel.origin: A Critical Hit — rolling a natural 20 on an attack, dealing extra damage (often double dice). The thrilling jackpot of combat rolls.
panel.mixology: Her natural 20 scored a crit, and the doubled damage felled the boss in one blow.
panel.examples: Short for 'critical hit'. Triggered by a natural 20, the crit is combat's big payoff — a moment of doubled damage and table-wide excitement.

panel.origin: The group of player characters adventuring together. The party is the team at the heart of the game, ideally balancing different classes and roles.
panel.mixology: The party of four — a fighter, wizard, cleric, and rogue — set out into the dungeon together.
panel.examples: From 'party' (a group with a shared purpose, as in a 'search party'). The adventuring party is the cooperative core of the tabletop experience.

panel.origin: Non-Player Character — anyone in the game world controlled by the DM rather than a player: shopkeepers, villains, allies, and quest-givers.
panel.mixology: The mysterious NPC in the tavern offered them a map in exchange for a dangerous favor.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'non-player character', borrowed across all of gaming. NPCs populate the world the players move through, voiced and run by the DM.

panel.origin: A preliminary meeting before a campaign begins, where the group sets expectations, creates characters, and agrees on tone, rules, and boundaries.
panel.mixology: At session zero, they agreed on the campaign's tone and drew up everyone's backstories together.
panel.examples: Named as the 'zeroth' session before play begins. A modern best practice for aligning the group's expectations and avoiding conflicts down the road.

panel.origin: A discrete challenge the party faces — a fight, a puzzle, a negotiation, a trap. Encounters are the building blocks of an adventure's action.
panel.mixology: The next encounter was a tense standoff with bandits that could turn to violence or talk.
panel.examples: From 'encounter' (a meeting, often hostile). Each distinct challenge — combat or otherwise — is an 'encounter', the unit DMs use to pace an adventure.

panel.origin: Custom content created by the DM or players rather than from official rulebooks — homemade monsters, items, rules, or entire worlds.
panel.mixology: The DM ran a homebrew world of her own design, full of custom monsters and lore.
panel.examples: From home-brewing (making your own beer). It captures the do-it-yourself spirit of crafting your own game material instead of relying on published books.

panel.origin: When a DM forces the party down a predetermined path, removing meaningful choice. A common criticism of overly controlling game-running.
panel.mixology: The players felt railroaded when every alternative they tried was mysteriously blocked.
panel.examples: From a train 'railroaded' onto fixed tracks. It describes a DM funneling players along a rigid plot, denying them the agency that makes RPGs special.

panel.origin: The treasure, gear, gold, and magic items the party earns from adventures. Distributing loot fairly is a perennial source of both joy and table drama.
panel.mixology: The dragon's hoard yielded enough loot to outfit the entire party in magic gear.
panel.examples: From 'loot' (plunder). The reward for victory — gold, weapons, and magic items — and the cause of countless good-natured (and not-so-good-natured) party arguments.

panel.origin: Optimizing a character to maximize strengths and minimize weaknesses, squeezing out peak mechanical power — sometimes at the expense of roleplay or balance.
panel.mixology: His min-maxed build hit like a truck but couldn't hold a conversation to save his life.
panel.examples: A compound of 'minimize' and 'maximize'. The min-maxer dumps unimportant stats to push key ones to the limit, chasing raw effectiveness over rounded character.

panel.origin: Slang for a player whose character solves every problem with violence and has no home, ties, or restraint — wandering the land killing and looting.
panel.mixology: The party devolved into murder hobos, attacking every NPC instead of talking to them.
panel.examples: A vivid compound: a homeless ('hobo') wanderer who murders their way through the world. A gently mocking term for a thoughtlessly violent play style.

panel.origin: Total Party Kill — when every player character dies in a single encounter. The catastrophic, campaign-ending (or restarting) wipeout every party dreads.
panel.mixology: A single misjudged fight against the dragon ended in a brutal TPK.
panel.examples: An acronym for 'total party kill'. The ultimate disaster — the whole party wiped out at once — feared, mourned, and sometimes laughed about for years after.

panel.origin: Using out-of-character knowledge (player knowledge) to make in-character decisions your character wouldn't actually know. Generally frowned upon as breaking immersion.
panel.mixology: It was metagaming for her to avoid the trap her character had no way of knowing about.
panel.examples: From 'meta' (above/beyond the game). Acting on knowledge from outside the fiction — that a monster is weak to fire, say — when the character couldn't know it.

panel.origin: A player who frequently invokes and argues the precise rules, often to their advantage. Helpful when the group's stuck, exhausting when it derails play.
panel.mixology: The rules lawyer paused the game for ten minutes to debate the exact wording of the spell.
panel.examples: A compound likening the player to a 'lawyer' arguing the letter of the 'rules'. Their precision can clarify or, when overdone, grind the fun to a halt.

panel.origin: A Critical Failure — rolling a natural 1, often causing an automatic, sometimes catastrophically funny failure. The dreaded flip side of the crit.
panel.mixology: His nat 1 crit-fail sent the arrow flying backward into his own ally.
panel.examples: Short for 'critical failure'. The natural 1's comic disaster — many tables add house rules turning it into spectacular, memorable mishaps.
